source.title: Functional dissociation of stimulus intensity encoding and predictive coding of pain in the insula

confidence_rationale: Direct fMRI dissociation of sensory-intensity encoding (posterior insula) from predictive-coding integration (anterior insula) in a probabilistic heat-pain paradigm. High source quality (Buchel lab, eLife, adequate N, autonomic measures replicate the BOLD finding). Maps cleanly onto REE's question: the z_harm_s analog is encoded elsewhere than where z_harm_a predictive integration happens. Slight ambiguity: Geuter does not directly test whether there are two independent forward models; it shows anatomical dissociation of consumers, which is consistent with shared or separate forward models upstream.
failure_signatures: Shared substrate is falsified if posterior insula (intensity) and anterior insula (integration) are driven by a single common predictor rather than two separable signal streams.
A unified pain forecast that simply branches at readout would predict identical temporal signatures in both regions; Geuter shows divergent response patterns.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Anterior insula responses follow a predictive-coding pattern (expectation + prediction error integration), whereas posterior insula encodes stimulus intensity.
mapping.ree_translation: The consumer of z_harm_a (affective PE integration, SD-032b) is anatomically and functionally distinct from the consumer of z_harm_s (intensity encoding, sensory-discriminative stream). The paper supports a split at the consumer side. Whether the upstream forward models are shared is not directly tested.
mapping.mapping_caveat: Geuter is silent on the forward-model architecture upstream. It shows two different consumers of pain signals, not two independent predictors. A single forward model with two readouts is consistent with these data.
mapping.source_context: Human fMRI, probabilistic heat-pain cued paradigm, healthy adults.

confidence_components.notes: Healthy human fMRI -> REE computational agent. Low-to-moderate transfer risk because the architectural question (where does integration happen relative to intensity) is substrate-agnostic.
